behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is local backoff math/state update, with no required module handoff or I/O. Higher levels were rejected because behavior text is only about delay progression and cap ("doubles" and "up to a configured maximum"), not transport/timer infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Test a pure backoff calculator (or retry-state object) with consecutive failure counts; assert delays follow 2^n growth and clamp at max.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: This is a module-boundary behavior: HTTP response classification + retry policy calculation together. Unit may miss mismatches in response shape/header parsing across modules. System rejected because behavior does not require real network/OS; Agentic/Workflow rejected because no AI step exists.
behaviors.test_description: Wire retry coordinator with a stub transport returning a realistic 429 (and optional retry headers). Assert it is classified as rate-limit and computed delay matches policy/header rules.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration is required to exercise response handoff between transport adapter and retry policy.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: Real failure appears in orchestration across retry loop, scheduler, and request execution. A single-function unit test can’t prove re-dispatch of the original request end-to-end. System rejected because in-process stub transport and fake timers can reproduce this failure.
behaviors.test_description: Use fake timers plus stub transport that returns rate-limit once, then success. Call `call()` once; advance time; assert transport invoked twice with equivalent request payload and final resolved value is the success body.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: Need retry-loop exhaustion + error translation path together; bug often occurs at boundary between transport errors and public API error mapping. System rejected because no real HTTP/OS needed to verify typed error contract. Agentic/Workflow rejected (no model output).
behaviors.test_description: Stub transport to always return rate-limit; configure max retries low; execute `call()` and assert rejection is instance/type `QuotaExhaustedError` with expected metadata, not raw/generic error.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly spans multiple local modules/state machines (retry chain + quota tracking). Unit is too low for this cross-module termination invariant. System rejected because deterministic in-process transport + fake clock can expose infinite-loop/termination bugs without real network.
behaviors.test_description: Run `call()` with always-rate-limited stub, bounded retries, and fake timers; assert total attempts equals expected bound, promise settles with `QuotaExhaustedError`, and no further scheduled retries remain.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test directly exercises multi-module termination logic, matching Integration boundary.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: Failure is shared-state handoff across concurrent callers and quota manager, which is beyond a single local function. System rejected because behavior text scopes to "same process" and can be reproduced with in-process concurrency; no real OS/network primitive is required.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate one client/quota manager; fire many concurrent `call()` invocations with stub transport; assert aggregate consumed tokens/attempt permissions are enforced globally (not per-caller) and observed throttling reflects one shared bucket.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: This is queueing behavior across submission API, wait list, and dispatcher modules; ordering bugs emerge at module interaction under async scheduling. System rejected because a controlled in-process queue + fake timers/stub transport can reveal FIFO violations. No AI dependency, so Agentic/Workflow rejected.
behaviors.test_description: Enqueue multiple calls quickly while quota blocks immediate send; release capacity stepwise; assert transport dispatch order matches submission order identifiers.
